description: The simplest and most underrated Indomie preparation. Boiled, drained, then dry fried in oil with onion and the seasoning pack until the edges crisp slightly. No vegetables, no extras — just noodles done perfectly.

ingredients.name: Indomie instant noodles
ingredients.note: Any flavour. Original or Chicken recommended for dry fry.

ingredients.name: vegetable oil
ingredients.note: Slightly more oil than stir fry — you need enough to coat and crisp the noodles.

ingredients.name: onion
ingredients.note: Thin slices that will crisp and caramelise alongside the noodles.

ingredients.name: scotch bonnet pepper
ingredients.note: Optional. Adds heat without adding moisture.

ingredients.name: indomie seasoning sachets
ingredients.note: Powder sachet only for dry fry — no liquid sachet. Liquid adds moisture and prevents crisping.

ingredients.name: egg
ingredients.note: Cracked directly into the noodles and scrambled in for a richer version.

ingredients.name: butter
ingredients.note: Added at the very end. Makes the noodles glossy and rich.

steps.title: Boil and Drain Noodles
steps.instruction: Speed and timing are everything in this step — you are deliberately undercooking the noodles because they will finish in the hot pan. Bring a small pot of water to a full rolling boil over high heat. Add the noodles and nothing else — no seasoning, no salt, no oil. Boil for exactly 90 seconds and no longer. Drain immediately into a sieve and shake it vigorously several times to remove as much surface water as possible. Spread the noodles out on a plate or tray and leave them to steam and dry for 1 full minute before they go anywhere near the pan. The drier the noodle surface, the crispier the edges will get.
micro_steps: Fill a small pot with just enough water to submerge the noodles — about 2 cups
Bring to a full rolling boil over high heat
Add the dry noodle blocks — do not add any seasoning, salt, or oil
Start a timer for exactly 90 seconds the moment the noodles go in
Use chopsticks or a fork to gently separate the noodle blocks as they soften
At 90 seconds, drain immediately into a sieve — do not wait
Shake the sieve vigorously 5–6 times to remove as much surface water as possible
Spread the drained noodles on a plate and leave uncovered for 1 minute to steam dry
prep_notes: Have all ingredients ready before boiling — this recipe moves extremely fast once the noodles are in the pan
Open the seasoning sachets and set aside the powder sachet only — do not use the liquid sachet in dry fry
Slice the onion and scotch bonnet before boiling the noodles so they are ready to go immediately
steps.sensory_cue: Noodles at 90 seconds should be pliable but noticeably firm — significantly undercooked compared to eating straight from the pot. They should look slightly translucent in places rather than the fully opaque, soft look of ready-to-eat noodles. The surface should not feel wet or sticky when you touch a strand — if it does, they need more time in the sieve.
steps.failure_risk: Wet noodles steam in the pan instead of frying and you never get the crispy edges that define dry fry — the whole point of the technique. Drain very thoroughly and allow the full 1-minute steam-dry rest on the plate. If you are using 2 packs, spread them in as thin a layer as possible so the steam escapes evenly.

steps.title: Fry the Onion
steps.instruction: Heat the vegetable oil in a wide pan over high heat until it shimmers. Add the thinly sliced onion and scotch bonnet if using. Leave completely undisturbed for 1 full minute — let the onion make contact with the hot oil and develop colour and crispiness at the edges before you touch it. Then stir and fry for another 30 seconds. The goal is golden, slightly crispy onion with caramelised edges — not just softened and translucent. This textural onion is what gives dry fry its characteristic bite.
micro_steps: Heat 1½ tablespoons of vegetable oil in a wide pan over high heat
Wait until the oil shimmers visibly
Add the thinly sliced onion in a single layer
Add the sliced scotch bonnet if using
Leave completely undisturbed for 1 full minute — do not stir
After 1 minute, the edges of the onion should be golden-brown and slightly crispy
Stir once and fry for another 30 seconds
The onion should look golden with crispy edges — not pale and soft
steps.sensory_cue: The onion edges should be golden-brown and slightly crispy — you should see some colour variation between the browned edges and the more translucent centre of each slice. The oil itself should look slightly golden from the onion caramelisation. It should smell sweet and slightly smoky — the smell of onion hitting very hot oil.
steps.failure_risk: Soft, pale, fully translucent onion adds no texture or visual interest to dry fry. The whole point is to get some colour and a slight crispness on the onion before the noodles go in — if it looks pale and soft, give it another 30 seconds over high heat before adding the noodles.

steps.title: Dry Fry the Noodles
steps.instruction: Add the drained, dried noodles to the pan with the onion and spread them out into an even layer using a spatula or tongs. Now leave them completely undisturbed for 1 full minute — this is the moment the bottom layer of noodles makes contact with the hot pan surface and begins to crisp at the edges. After 1 minute, toss and fold the noodles, then spread them out again into an even layer and leave for another minute. Repeat this sit-and-toss cycle two more times. In the last cycle, sprinkle the powder seasoning sachet evenly over the noodles and toss thoroughly to coat every strand.
micro_steps: Add the drained noodles to the pan — spread into an even layer with tongs or a spatula
Leave completely undisturbed for 1 full minute
After 1 minute, toss and fold the noodles to bring the bottom layer to the top
Spread back into an even layer and leave for another 1 minute
Toss and fold again — you should see some strands starting to develop golden, crispy edges
Spread into an even layer one more time and leave for 1 minute
Open the powder seasoning sachet — do not use the liquid sachet
Sprinkle the powder evenly over the noodles
Toss thoroughly for 30 seconds to coat every strand with the seasoning
Taste a strand — it should be seasoned, slightly dry, and have some crispy edges
steps.sensory_cue: As the noodles fry, you will see and smell the difference — the bottom strands develop a slightly golden, crispy edge and the whole pan smells toasty and savoury rather than steamy and wet. The overall texture of the noodles should be noticeably drier and firmer than boiled noodles, with some strands clearly crisped and others still slightly softer. The seasoning should coat every strand evenly and smell unmistakably like Indomie.
steps.failure_risk: Constant stirring is the death of dry fry — it prevents any noodle strand from maintaining enough contact with the hot pan surface to crisp. The sit-and-toss method is the entire technique. If your pan is not hot enough, the noodles will steam in their own moisture rather than fry — increase the heat and make sure the pan was properly preheated before the noodles went in.

steps.title: Add Egg and Finish
steps.instruction: If adding an egg, push all the noodles to one side of the pan with your spatula to clear a small space. Crack the egg directly into the cleared space and let it sit undisturbed for about 15 seconds until the whites just begin to set at the edges. Then scramble it briefly in that spot and immediately fold the noodles back over and through it, mixing the partially set egg evenly through the noodles. If adding butter, add it now and toss until melted and coating every strand with a glossy sheen. Remove from heat immediately, taste once — the powder sachet should provide all the seasoning needed, but add the tiniest pinch of salt if it tastes flat. Serve and eat immediately.
micro_steps: If using egg, push the noodles to one side of the pan to clear a small space
Crack the egg into the cleared space
Leave for 15 seconds until the whites begin to set at the edges
Scramble the egg briefly in the pan with a fork or spatula
Fold the noodles back over the egg and mix through immediately
If using butter, add ½ tsp now and toss until fully melted and glossy
Remove the pan from heat immediately
Taste one strand — add a tiny pinch of salt only if it genuinely tastes flat
Transfer to a plate and eat immediately while the edges are still crispy
steps.sensory_cue: Finished dry fry should have a satisfying mix of textures — some strands soft and chewy, other edges noticeably crispy and golden. The overall appearance should be golden and glossy, not pale or wet. The smell is toasty, savoury, and deeply satisfying — the Indomie seasoning mixed with the slightly charred noodle edges is one of the most comforting smells in quick cooking.
steps.failure_risk: The crispy texture fades quickly as the noodles sit and begin to absorb ambient moisture — within a few minutes of leaving the pan, the crispiness softens significantly. Eat immediately for the full experience. Do not cover the plate or the steam will destroy the texture entirely.
